story:
Falling in love is easy. It’s everything else that’s tricky. The wooing, the courting. I learned that the hard way.

I met Gemma when I was nineteen, out by our spot—along the dry-stone wall below the MacDaniels’ barn. Duncan brought her along. “This is Gemma, my cousin,” he said. She had a halo of curly blonde hair, legs that went on, and a smile that looked right at you.

She asked my name. Me. “I’m Jimmy,” I said, chest puffed like I’d grown two inches. I told her I helped my dad on the farm. She asked if I was coming to the dance next Saturday. I said I was. “I’ll see you there,” she said. And that was it. I was in love.

All week I floated. At home I pranced around our yellow kitchen. Mum, wrists deep in suds, said I needed something to wear. Dad looked me up and down. “A suit?” he said.
“A suit,” I said.
He sighed, then nodded. “Something from town.”

On the Saturday afternoon we drove down the rutted track, fields bright and clean with spring, lambs wobbling on new legs. Dad patted old Bessie, the car. “Let’s hope she holds out,” he said. A knot formed in my stomach. Timing mattered.

At the shop a sign on the door said, Back in five. We waited. Five turned to twenty-five. The knot tightened. Dad went round the back. A voice called, “Sorry—wee problem with the wain.” The woman opened up for us anyway, breathless and kind.

I tried on suits while Dad held up horrors for a laugh—a shiny blue number with a frilled shirt.
“No,” I said, grinning, and chose a smart grey that made me look, I thought, older than nineteen.
“Looks grand on you,” the woman said.

We got my hair tidied at the barber, then headed home. Suit on, hair done. Two hours to go.

“You look gorgeous, son,” Mum said, straightening my tie. She handed me a bunch of flowers from her garden. I sighed, but took them. Dad would drive me and, because Bessie had been sulking lately, he said he’d wait outside to make sure we got there and back.

I sang “I’m going to see Gemma” under my breath as we went out to the car. Bessie sputtered, shuddered, died. Inside, I died a little too. We checked under the bonnet. Nothing obvious—just the stubborn silence of old metal. Dad rang a mechanic in Dumfries. “Tea,” Mum said, practical as ever, and we waited, me watching the lane until the van finally rolled up, slow as you like.

“My son has a dance to go to,” Dad told him straight away. The mechanic took one look at my face and got to work. “There you go,” he said at last, wiping his hands. We thanked him and flew.

We pulled up not too late, but not smugly on time either. “We might have pulled this off,” I said. Dad squeezed my shoulder. The hall windows spilled warm light onto the gravel. I took one flower from Mum’s bunch, left the rest on the sill, checked my breath, straightened my suit.

“Thanks, Dad,” I said.
“I’ll be here,” he said, settling back behind the wheel.

I opened the door.

And there she was—Gemma—snogging my friend Rory beneath the paper bunting. Everything inside me stopped for a beat—heart, breath, all of it. My eyes stayed wide. My mouth too. The flower slipped from my fingers and hit the floor without a sound I could hear over the music. I turned and walked—then ran—back out into the cool night.

Dad was still there, engine ticking, radio low. I got in and cried into my sleeve.

“She was… she was with someone else,” I managed.

Dad didn’t say much. He rummaged in his pocket and handed me a tissue.

“She was beautiful though, Dad,” I said finally.

“Aye, son. Aye.”

Falling in love is easy. It’s everything else that’s tricky. I know that now.
